elements.sent-2411-001: Although recent diffusion-based single-step super-resolution methods achieve better performance as compared to SinSR~\cite{SinSR , they are computationally complex.}
elements.sent-2411-002: To improve the performance of SinSR, we investigate preserving the high-frequency detail features during super-resolution (SR) because the downgraded images lack detailed information.
elements.sent-2411-003: For this purpose, we introduce a high-frequency perceptual loss by utilizing an invertible neural network (INN) pretrained on the ImageNet dataset.
elements.sent-2411-004: Different feature maps of pretrained INN produce different high-frequency aspects of an image.
elements.sent-2411-005: During the training phase, we impose to preserve the high-frequency features of super-resolved and ground truth (GT) images that improve the SR image quality during inference.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we also utilize the Jenson-Shannon divergence between GT and SR images in the pretrained DINO-v2 embedding space to match their distribution.
elements.sent-2411-007: By introducing the high- frequency preserving loss and distribution matching constraint in the single-step diffusion-based SR (HF-Diff), we achieve a state-of-the-art CLIPIQA score in the benchmark RealSR, RealSet65, DIV2K-Val, and ImageNet datasets.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, the experimental results in several datasets demonstrate that our high-frequency perceptual loss yields better SR image quality than LPIPS and VGG-based perceptual losses.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our code will be released in the repository at { \url{https://github.com/shoaib-sami/HF-Diff .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.25\linewidth, bb=0 0 800 563]{front_image-v1-compressed.pdf} \caption{The visual and image quality comparison among the recent SOTA approaches (OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff} and SinSR~\cite{SinSR}). We achieve the SOTA image perceptual quality (CLIPIQA~\cite{clipiqa}) score. } \label{fig:abc}
elements.sent-2411-010: Super-resolution (SR) is a well-known ill-posed problem in low-level computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-011: The primary goal of super-resolution is to improve the detailed information in degraded images.
elements.sent-2411-012: The degradation of the image can occur due to downsampling, blurring, the effect of noise, and real-world degradation factors~\cite{SR_survey_TPAMI .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The degraded image contains structural information and lacks high-frequency detail information of an object~\cite{s3diff,matching_acm .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The main goal of SR is to recover the missing information from the degraded image.
elements.sent-2411-015: Researchers use the generative adversarial network~\cite{SR_CNN , normalizing flow~\cite{srflow , auto-regressive~\cite{autoregressive_model_SR , transformer~\cite{transformer_SR , and diffusion model-based algorithms~\cite{saharia_iterative_refinement to solve the SR problem.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, diffusion-based SR outperforms the other techniques in several metrices~\cite{clipiqa,lpips,musiq .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Our paper aims to improve the single-step diffusion-based super-resolution algorithms for better performance than state-of-the-art (SOTA) SR methods~\cite{SinSR,oseDiff , which is crucial for different commercial~\cite{commercial-0,commercial-1 , academic~\cite{academic-1,academic-2 , medical science~\cite{medical-sr-tpami , and military~\cite{military-1,military-2 applications.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Regarding model convergence, diffusion model-based SR algorithms~\cite{resshift,SinSR,oseDiff,Exposurediffusion,srdiff have advantages over the GAN-based SR~\cite{SR_CNN .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The initial idea of diffusion-based super-resolution, SR3~\cite{saharia_iterative_refinement , was proposed by employing low-resolution (LR) images inserted into the input of the denoiser in the diffusion model (DDPM~\cite{ddpm ).}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, SR3 performance drops if the degraded image comes from an unknown distribution.
elements.sent-2411-021: To overcome this issue, SR3+~\cite{sr3_plus_sahak-denoising was proposed using the self-supervised training strategy, a higher order degradation method, and noise conditioning augmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-022: After that, the pretrained diffusion model and its modified reverse path were introduced using the transition distribution from the LR image to generate SR~\cite{TPAMI_difface image.}
elements.sent-2411-023: These prior diffusion model-based SR algorithms require many computationally intensive sampling steps.
elements.sent-2411-024: ResShift~\cite{resshift was proposed to overcome the computational complexity, which needs only 15 sampling steps and performs better than the earlier diffusion-based SR approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In that paper, the authors state that the reverse diffusion process starts from a bicubically interpolated LR image, and in each reverse step, the residual of the LR and HR image is dynamically integrated.
elements.sent-2411-026: To improve the SR inference process, the authors of SinSR~\cite{SinSR utilized deterministic sampling, similar to DDIM~\cite{ddim in the teacher model.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Then, they distill the teacher diffusion SR process to a student model in a single-step by maintaining better SR image quality than the teacher model.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our method also distills teacher information to the student model to accelerate the sampling procedure.
elements.sent-2411-029: Recently, S3Diff~\cite{s3diff and OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff , utilized the pretrained text-to-image (T2I) model's information as a prior for the SR algorithm, and achieved better performance than SinSR~\cite{SinSR .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Moreover, SinSR lags slightly in another widely used image quality metric (MUSIQ~\cite{musiq ) in several approaches~\cite{oseDiff,bsrgan .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The CLIPQA~\cite{clipiqa can capture semantic and abstract perception inside the image using the pretrained CLIP~\cite{clip model.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, MUSIQ can capture the perceptual quality of an image in multiple granularities.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our paper aims to improve the generated SR image's semantic and abstract perception.
elements.sent-2411-034: To achieve this goal, we propose several novelties: i) we preserve the high-frequency features between super-resolution (SR) and ground-truth (GT) images, and ii) we match the distribution of GT and SR images during training.
elements.sent-2411-035: Adversarial learning and classifier guidance are widely used in the diffusion model community for better image generation~\cite{classifier-guidance,Adversarial-structure-diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-036: One approach to stabilizing adversarial learning is to minimize the Jenson-Shannon divergence~\cite{jensen-shannon between the distribution of perturbed original images and the diffusion model's generated images~\cite{Diffusion-GAN .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, in diffusion model literature, feature matching between real and generated image distributions has been extensively performed~\cite{tesuang_park_distribution_matching_diffusion,dmd_park,matching_acm,oseDiff .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff employs KL divergence to regularize the latent space of GT and SR image distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The authors~\cite{matching_acm introduced the nearest neighbor algorithm and a pretrained codebook to match the distorted low-resolution and SR image in embedding space.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Inspired by these methods, we minimize the Jenson-Shannon (JS) divergence between the ground-truth HR image and the corresponding SR image, which yields better image quality.
elements.sent-2411-041: For this purpose, we use the SOTA self-supervised DINO-v2~\cite{dinov2 pretrained encoder to extract embedding from GT and corresponding SR images and utilize JS divergence.}
elements.sent-2411-042: We propose a high-frequency perceptual loss to preserve the detail and fine-grained features in the student super-resolution model.
elements.sent-2411-043: There are different types of perceptual loss in the computer-vision literature~\cite{perceptual_loss_vgg,frequency_perceptual,edge-texture-loss-targeted-perceptual .}
elements.sent-2411-044: We design an invertible neural network~\cite{invertible_neural_network (INN) and train it on the ImageNet-1K~\cite{imagenet dataset to calculate high-frequency perceptual loss.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The INN-based high-frequency feature extractor is widely used in the visible-infrared image fusion literature~\cite{cddfuse,inn_arxiv,INN_2022visible .}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, to the best of our knowledge, we are pioneering the design of a high-frequency perceptual loss to preserve fine-grained and detailed features in image super-resolution.
elements.sent-2411-047: This pretrained high-frequency (HF) feature extractor calculates HF perceptual loss in the generated SR image and its corresponding GT image.
elements.sent-2411-048: By introducing high-frequency perceptual loss and distribution matching in the single-step super-resolution algorithm~\cite{SinSR , we achieve a SOTA CLIPIQA score by comparing recent SR algorithms~\cite{oseDiff,SinSR,resshift in four datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The main contributions of our proposed super-resolution algorithm are:.
elements.sent-2411-050: We propose a high-frequency preserving perceptual loss, which facilitates and enforces the network to maintain detail and fine-grained information.
elements.sent-2411-051: Our SR algorithm matches and aligns the GT and SR image distributions by minimizing the Jenson-Shannon divergence in the pretrained DINO-v2 embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art CLIPIQA score in the RealSet65~\cite{resshift , RealSR~\cite{realsr , ImageNet~\cite{imagenet , DIV2K-Val~\cite{div2k-val , and DrealSR~\cite{drealdataset datasets.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: In the DIV2K-Val and RealSR datasets, we achieve a better CLIPIQA~\cite{clipiqa score than the recent OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff algorithm.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Furthermore, our method improves the qualitative visual representation compared to SOTA SR approaches.
elements.sent-2411-055: Super-resolution is a well-known low-level computer vision problem widely used in many applications~\cite{SR_survey_TPAMI,SR_dong_2015 , such as surveillance~\cite{surveillance-1 , medical imaging~\cite{medical-sr-tpami , gaming~\cite{commercial-0 , virtual reality~\cite{virtual-reality , photography~\cite{photography , face recognition~\cite{face-SR , etc. After the evolution of AlexNet~\cite{alexnet , researchers implemented deep learning-based super-resolution approaches~\cite{SR_dong_2015,perceptual_loss_vgg .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Following that, the generative adversarial network (GAN) evolved, and the GAN-based SR algorithms~\cite{sr-gan,esrgan,bsrgan were mainstream in the computer vision community~\cite{SR_dong_2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The SR-GAN~\cite{sr-gan , ESRGAN~\cite{esrgan , and RankSRGAN~\cite{ranksrgan are some well-known GAN-based super-resolution algorithms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The invertible neural network-based SRFlow~\cite{srflow outperformed the GAN-based SR algorithms in 2020.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Furthermore, the transformer~\cite{transformer is the dominant network for natural language processing, image classification, and detection, which facilitates the researchers to implement the transformer in super-resolution~\cite{transformer_SR .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: In addition, the denoising diffusion model beats the GAN in different perceptual metrics in the generative computer vision field~\cite{classifier-guidance .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The first denoising diffusion model-based SR algorithm was introduced in 2021~\cite{saharia_iterative_refinement .}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, the diffusion-based SR algorithms initially faced slow sampling speeds and many inference steps~\cite{saharia_iterative_refinement,resshift .}
elements.sent-2411-063: ResShift~\cite{resshift was first algorithm able to sample high-quality super-resolution using a denoising diffusion model in only fifteen steps to overcome the slow sampling process.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Recently, researchers~\cite{SinSR, oseDiff,s3diff have successfully inferred the diffusion-based super-resolution in a single-step.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Our paper improves the single-step diffusion-based SR algorithm that outperforms SOTA performance in some image quality metrics.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Analysis of Detailed Information in the Super-Resolution
elements.sent-2411-066: In the super-resolution literature, pretrained VGG-16-based perceptual loss~\cite{perceptual_loss_vgg is widely used to improve the SR image quality.}
elements.sent-2411-067: In addition, Sims et al.~\cite{frequency_perceptual introduce frequency domain perceptual loss using discrete cosine transform~\cite{dct in the ground-truth and the SR image.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Alongside perceptual loss, adversarial loss~\cite{SR_dong_2015 , neural texture transfer~\cite{neural-texture-transfer,texture-transformer , edge smoothness prior~\cite{edge-prior-hallucination , gradient profile prior~\cite{gradient-profile-prior , edge and texture losses~\cite{edge-texture-loss-targeted-perceptual,edge-loss are used to achieve fine-grained information in the SR.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: For recovering texture information in the SR, the spatial feature transform and semantic segmentation are used in the CNN network~\cite{recovering-texture-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: In the subsequent section, we will discuss deterministic sampling~\cite{ddim in the teacher model~\cite{SinSR and then delve into how our algorithm performs high-frequency perceptual loss and distribution matching.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We also discuss other losses, e.g., distillation and inverse loss.
elements.sent-2411-072: In this section, we will first discuss deterministic sampling in the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-073: Then, we will elaborate on the proposed high-frequency perceptual loss and distribution alignment of the SR algorithm close to ground-truth image distribution.
elements.sent-2411-074: The earlier work for non-Markovian denoising deterministic sampling was introduced in the DDIM~\cite{ddim paper.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In the super-resolution literature, the SinSR~\cite{SinSR was pioneered in employing deterministic sampling.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Inspired by SinSR, we employ deterministic sampling.
elements.sent-2411-077: The reverse deterministic sampling process $ q(x_{t-1 |x_t, x_0, y)$ can be expressed by: \begin{equation}q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0, y)= \delta(g_\theta,x_t,y,t) ,\end{equation} where $\delta$ represents the unit impulse function, $y$ is the low-resolution image, $x_0$ is the ground-truth image and $g_\theta$ is a pretrained ResShift~\cite{resshift SR model.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the reverse process, the DDIM can calculate $x_{t-1}$ from $x_{t}$ using following equation: \begin{equation}{x}_{t-1}=a_t g_\theta(x_t,y,t)+ b_t x_t + c_t y , \label{eq:bar}\end{equation} where $a_t$, $b_t$, and $c_t$ are hyper-parameters.
elements.sent-2411-079: The detailed derivation of the hyper-parameters is described in the SinSR~\cite{SinSR paper.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The deterministic teacher model is illustrated in ~\figurename ~\ref{fig:hd-diff .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Where $\mathcal{G _{\theta}$ is the T-step deterministic teacher network which predicts super-resolution output $\hat{x _0$ from $x_T$ state.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: We utilize the student model $\mathcal{G _{\hat{\theta }$ that learns the SR process from the teacher network ($\mathcal{G _{\theta}$) in a single-step.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: We utilized the vanilla distillation loss, consistency preserving, and inverse regularization losses from the SinSR~\cite{SinSR .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The vanilla distillation loss can be described as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{distill} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(x_T, y,T), \mathcal{G}_{\theta}(x_T, y)), \label{eq:matching}\end{equation} vanilla distillation facilitates the student model learning of the T-step teacher SR model in a single-step.
elements.sent-2411-085: The student model ($\mathcal{G _{\hat{\theta }$) learns the deterministic teacher ($\mathcal{G _{\theta}$) mapping in a single step by employing vanilla distillation.}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{hf-diff-v2.pdf} \caption{The training framework of HF-Diff. The low-resolution image is super-resolved by the multi-step deterministic teacher model ($\mathcal{G}_{\theta}$). A student model ($\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}$) learns the deterministic teacher model by optimizing distill, high-frequency perceptual, distribution matching JS divergence, inverse, and consistency preserving losses. } \label{fig:hd-diff}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Matching the Super-Resolved and Ground-Truth Image Distribution
elements.sent-2411-086: In the self-supervised learning benchmark, DINO-v2~\cite{dinov2 achieves promising performance, which interests us in extracting features of super-resolution (SR) and ground-truth (GT) images into the pretrained DINO-v2 space.}
elements.sent-2411-087: We introduce aligning and matching the probability distribution of SR and GT images in DINO-v2 space.
elements.sent-2411-088: We align the probability distribution by using the Jenson-Shannon divergence~\cite{jensen-shannon .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Let $P_{SR}$ and $ P_{GT}$ represent the probability distributions of the HR and SR images in the DINO-v2 embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-090: Then, the Jenson-Shannon divergence Loss ($\mathcal{L _{JS-DINO}$) can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{L}_{JS-DINO} = \text{JSD}(P_{SR} || P_{GT}) =\frac{1}{2} \left[ \text{KL}(P_{SR} || P_{Avg}) + \text{KL}(P_{GT} || P_{Avg}) \right] , \end{split}\end{equation} where $P_{Avg (x) = \frac{1 {2 \left[(P_{SR (x) + P_{GT (x) \right]$ and $KL$ is Kullback-Leibler Divergence.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} L_{JS-DINO} = JSD(P_{SR} || P_{GT}) =\frac{1}{2} \left[ KL(P_{SR} || P_{Avg}) + KL(P_{GT} || P_{Avg}) \right] , \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-091: The well-known implementation of perceptual loss is CNN-based~\cite{perceptual_loss_vgg,lpips framework; nevertheless, CNN does not provide lossless, invertible mapping between input and output.}
elements.sent-2411-092: However, the invertible neural network (INN)~\cite{invertible_neural_network can maintain these properties and is widely used for high-frequency feature extraction~\cite{cddfuse,INN_2022visible,inn_arxiv .}}
elements.sent-2411-093: In the image super-resolution domain, high-frequency image features are crucial because downgraded images lack detailed information.
elements.sent-2411-094: We introduce an invertible neural network-based high-frequency perceptual loss to compare and force the super-resolution algorithm to preserve the high-frequency (HF) feature~\cite{cddfuse .}
elements.sent-2411-095: For this purpose, we use an INN-based classifier (the details are shown in the Algorithm \ref{alg:inn ) pretrained on the ImageNet~\cite{imagenet dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: For calculating HF perceptual loss, we preserve the spatial dimension of the feature map in the same way as the input.
elements.sent-2411-097: The different channels of INN can extract different perceptual detailed information about an image.
elements.sent-2411-098: We improve detailed information and perceptual image quality~\cite{clipiqa by utilizing HF-perceptual loss.}
elements.sent-2411-099: The invertible neural network consists of K numbers of invertible modules.
elements.sent-2411-100: The $(k+1)$-th invertible module can be denoted as~\cite{cddfuse : \begin{equation}\label{equ:INN-1} \begin{aligned} \psi_{k+1}\left[n+1\!:\!N\right] =\psi_{k}\left[n+1\!:\!N\right]+\mathcal{I}_1\left(\psi_{k}\left[1\!:\!n\right]\right), \psi_{k+1}\left[1\!:\!n\right] =\psi_{k}\left[1\!:\!n\right] \odot \exp \left(\mathcal{I}_2\left(\psi_{k+1}\left[n+1\!:\!N\right]\right)\right) +\mathcal{I}_3\left(\psi_{k+1}\left[n+1\!:\!N\right]\right), \psi_{k+1} =\mathcal{CAT}\left (\psi_{k+1}\left[1\!:\!n\right],\psi_{k+1} \left[n+1\!:\!N\right]\right], \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\odot$ is the Hadamard product, $\psi_{k \left[1\!:\!n\right]\!\in\!\mathbb{R ^{h\times w\times n}$ is the 1st to the $n$-th channels of the input feature for the $k$-th invertible layer ($k=1,\cdots,K$), the channel concatenation is denoted as $\mathcal{CAT (\cdot)$ and $\mathcal{I _i$ ($i=1,\cdots,3$) are three arbitrary mapping functions in each invertible layer.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: We utilize a two-layer CNN as an arbitrary mapping function in the invertible module.
elements.sent-2411-102: The invertible module is illustrated in \figurename ~\ref{fig:INN .}
elements.sent-2411-103: The ImageNet pretrained high-frequency feature extractor can be denoted as $\mathcal{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-104: The HF-perceptual loss can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{HF-perceptual} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE}(\mathcal{H}(x_0),\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(x_T))), \label{eq:reverse}\end{equation} where GT and degraded LR image are $x_0$ and $x_T$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-105: The feature maps provide different aspects of the detailed features of a sample image.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Invertible_final_Diff.pdf} \caption{The illustration of an invertible module. The high-frequency feature extractor consists of K numbers of cascaded invertible modules~\cite{cddfuse}. The invertible module has an affine coupling layer~\cite{real_nvp} consisting of scaling and translation functions and a Hadamard product.} \label{fig:INN}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Distribution_matching_v2.drawio.pdf} \caption{Depiction of distribution matching between ground-truth (GT) and super-resolved (SR) images. The distribution of GT and SR are matched by adopting Jenson-Shannon divergence into the pretrained DINO-v2 embedding space. The distribution matching constraint guides the student model in generating a more realistic SR image.} \label{fig:js}
elements.sent-2411-106: In the regularization loss~\cite{SinSR , the student network ($\mathcal{G _{\hat{\theta }$) learns inverse mapping ($\hat{x _T$) and generates synthetic ground-truth ($\hat{x _0$) images.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: The inverse mapping loss can be denoted as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{inv} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(\mathcal{G}_{\theta}(x_T, y), y, 0), x_T). \label{eq:reverse}\end{equation} The consistency preserving loss~\cite{SinSR ($\mathcal{L _{Con}$) preserves the ground-truth information.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: First, the student model maps $x_0$ to $\hat{x _T$ and then again maps to $\hat{x _0=\mathcal{G _{\hat{\theta (\mathcal{G _{\hat{\theta (x_0, y, 0), y, T)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The consistency preserving loss ($\mathcal{L _{Con}$) minimizes the mean square error between $\hat{x _0$ and ${x _0$. \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{L}_{Con} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(x_0, y, 0), y, T), x_0), \end{split} \label{eq:gt_loss}\end{equation} where, $\mathcal{G _{\hat{\theta }$ is the student model.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The student model parameter $\hat{\theta}$ is initialized from the teacher parameter $\theta$ for faster convergence during training.
elements.sent-2411-112: Our proposed framework optimizes the previously described five training objectives.
elements.sent-2411-113: The overall objective ($\mathcal{L _{Total}$) can be denoted as: \begin{fleqn \begin{equation}L_{Total} = L_{distill} + \eta_1 \cdot L_{HF-Perceptual} + \eta_2 \cdot L_{JS-DINO} \notag \quad + L_{inv} + L_{Con}, \label{eq:total-objective}\end{equation} \end{fleqn where $\eta_1 $, and $\eta_2$ are hyper-parameter.}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: The overall of the proposed HF-Diff is described in the Algorithm~\ref{alg:training and \figurename~\ref{fig:hd-diff .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{{ Training HF-Diff}} \begin{minipage}{1\linewidth} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE T step DDIM teacher model ($\mathcal{G}_\theta$) and single-step student model ($\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}$). \REQUIRE Paired training set $(X, Y)$, where $X$ and $Y$ are the ground-truth and low-resolution image sets, respectively \STATE $\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}$ is initialized by utilizing the pretrained model ($\mathcal{G}_\theta$). \WHILE{not converged} \STATE sample $x_0, y \sim (X, Y)$ \STATE sample $\zeta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \kappa^2 {\eta_T} \mathbf{I})$ \STATE $x_T = y+\zeta$ \STATE $\hat{x}_{0}=\mathcal{G}_\theta(x_T, y, T)$ \STATE $\mathcal{L}_{distill} = L_{MSE}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(x_T, y, T), \hat{x}_0)$ \STATE $\mathcal{L}_{inv} = L_{MSE}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(\hat{x}_0, y, 0), x_T)$ \STATE $\hat{x}_T = \mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(x_0, y, 0)$, \STATE $\mathcal{L}_{Con} = L_{MSE}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(\hat{x}_T, y, T), x_0)$ \STATE $\mathcal{L}_{HF-Perceptual} = L_{MSE}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(\hat{x}_T, y, T), x_0)$ \STATE $\mathcal{L}_{JS-DINO} = L_{JS-Div.}(\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}(\hat{x}_T, y, T), x_0)$ \STATE $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{distill}+\mathcal{L}_{inv}+\mathcal{L}_{Con}+\mathcal{L}_{HF-Perceptual}+\mathcal{L}_{JS-DINO}$ \STATE Perform a gradient descent step on $\nabla_{\hat{\theta}} \mathcal{L} $ \ENDWHILE \RETURN The student model $\mathcal{G}_{\hat{\theta}}$. \end{algorithmic} \end{minipage} \label{alg:training}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{{ Pretraining of High-Frequency Feature Extractor}} 1{ \begin{minipage}{1\linewidth} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE { Invertible module ($\psi_{k}$)-based feature extractor, CNN module ($\mathcal{C}_l$), fully connected layers (FC), shallow residual-block-encoder ($\mathcal{RB}$) for increasing feature maps from 3 to N.} \REQUIRE ImageNet training set $(\bar{Z})$ \WHILE{not converged} \STATE sample $\bar{z} \sim (\bar{Z})$ \STATE $z_1$ = $\mathcal{RB}$($\bar{z}$) \FOR {$k = 1, 2, ..., K$} \STATE $z_{k+1}=\psi_{k}(z_{k})$ \ENDFOR \STATE $\hat{y}_1 = z_K$ \FOR {$l = 1, 2, 3,....L$} \STATE $\hat{y}_{l+1}=\mathcal{C}_{k}(\hat{y}_{l})$ \ENDFOR $y_{score}$ = Soft-max( FC$(\hat{y}_{L})$) \STATE $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{Cross-Entropy} (y_{score},y_{class})$ \STATE Perform a gradient descent step on $\nabla_{(\mathcal{RB}, \psi ,\mathcal{C},FC) } \mathcal{L} $ \ENDWHILE \RETURN The embedding of the K-th invertible module ($z_K$). \end{algorithmic} \end{minipage}} \label{alg:inn}
elements.sent-2411-116: We pursue the same model architecture and parameter setup as \cite{resshift,SinSR .}
elements.sent-2411-117: We fine-tune the SinSR~\cite{SinSR model with two additional novel objectives.}
elements.sent-2411-118: We train the HF-Diff model on ImageNet~\cite{imagenet dataset by following the identical procedure same as ResShift~\cite{resshift and SinSR~\cite{SinSR .}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Our paper pursues the real-world degradation pipeline from RealESRGAN~\cite{Real-esrgan .}
elements.sent-2411-121: We train a high-frequency feature extractor (based on an invertible neural network~\cite{cddfuse ) alongside convolutional and fully-connected layers to calculate HF-perceptual loss.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Initially, we use a shallow residual block~\cite{resnet to expand the image feature map from 3 to $N(=128)$.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The N channel of an image then passes through an invertible neural network.
elements.sent-2411-124: We take the output from the high-frequency feature extractor to calculate HF-perceptual loss.
elements.sent-2411-125: This network is trained on the ImageNet~\cite{imagenet dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-126: We train this model for 20 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-4 with a batch size of 32 and an exponential scheduler with a factor of 0.95 in every 5000 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-127: This model is optimized by Adam~\cite{adam optimizer.. Compared methods.}
elements.sent-2411-128: We analyze the performance of our proposed method with several super-resolution algorithms, e.g., StableSR-s200~\cite{stable-sr , RealSR-JPEG~\cite{realsr-jpeg , DiffBIR-s50~\cite{diffbir , SeeSR-s50~\cite{seesr , OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff , PASD-s20~\cite{pasd , ESRGAN~\cite{esrgan , ResShift~\cite{resshift , SinSR~\cite{SinSR , BSRGAN~\cite{bsrgan , SwinIR~\cite{swinir , RealESRGAN~\cite{Real-esrgan , DASR~\cite{DASR , and LDM~\cite{ldm .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: We also evaluate and compare the inference time of ours with ResShift~\cite{resshift , SinSR~\cite{SinSR , and OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff .. Metrics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: We employ PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS~\cite{lpips metrics for performance analysis in the testing dataset with reference images.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Furthermore, we also utilize three widely used non-reference image quality metrics to evaluate SR images' realism and semantic coherence.
elements.sent-2411-132: These three image quality metrics are CLIPIQA~\cite{clipiqa , MUSIQ~\cite{musiq , and NIQE~\cite{niqe .. Training load.}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: We train the HF-Diff network for 20K iterations with a batch size of 32.
elements.sent-2411-134: For this purpose, we initialized SinSR's pretrained weight.
elements.sent-2411-135: The convergence of our algorithm (HF-Diff) is much faster; it takes about 20 hours.

elements.sent-2411-137: We evaluate the performance of our proposed HF-Diff in the three real-world datasets RealSR~\cite{realsr , RealSet65~\cite{resshift and DrealSR~\cite{drealdataset .}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: The RealSet65 dataset contains 65 low-resolution images accumulated from different datasets and the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-139: The RealSR dataset has 100 real images captured by the `Nikon D810' and the `Canon 5D3' cameras.
elements.sent-2411-140: We investigate the image perceptual quality of HF-Diff with other state-of-the-art super-resolution algorithms in Table~\ref{tab:real_testing ~and~\ref{tab-osed .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: From Table~\ref{tab:real_testing , we achieve the best CLIPIQA~\cite{clipiqa score among widely used GAN, Transformer, and diffusion model-based SR algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Furthermore, HF-Diff achieves the second-best MUSIQ~\cite{musiq score among different SR algorithms and better than the recent SinSR~\cite{SinSR methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: We also analyze the image quality metrics, e.g., NIQE, MUSIQ, and CLIPIQA, of the different diffusion model-based approaches with ours on DrealSR~\cite{drealdataset , and RealSR~\cite{oseDiff datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: In the RealSR dataset, HF-Diff achieves the best CLIPIQA score compared to others.
elements.sent-2411-145: However, in the DrealSR dataset, our approach gets slightly diminutive results.
elements.sent-2411-146: Furthermore, in the NIQE and MUSIQ metrics, SeeSR~\cite{seesr and PASD~\cite{pasd achieve better performance than ours because these are multi-step diffusion-based approaches that yield slightly better texture than a single-step inference~\cite{oseDiff .}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: We investigate the reference-based fidelity metrics (PSNR, SSIM) and non-reference-based image quality metrics (NIQE, MUSIQ, CLIPIQA) in the ImageNet-Test~\cite{imagenet and DIV2K-Val~\cite{div2k-val datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: From Table~\ref{tab:imagenet_testing , our method achieves the best MUSIQ and CLIPIQA scores compared to the nine other SR approaches in the ImageNet-Test dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Our HF-Diff achieves the second-best LPIPS score.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, ours lags slightly in PSNR and SSIM metrics.
elements.sent-2411-152: We also find from Table~\ref{tab-osed that ours achieves the second-best CLIPIQA score compared to the other seven diffusion-based SR approaches in the DIV2K-Val dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-153: However, in NIQE and MUSIQ metrics, the SeeSR~\cite{seesr and PASD~\cite{pasd achieve better performance.. Qualitative comparisons.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: We visually compare our HF-Diff with recently introduced OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff , SinSR~\cite{SinSR , and other five super-resolution methods~\cite{bsrgan,Real-esrgan,swinir,ldm,resshift .}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: In three real-world samples, ours, OSEDiff, and RealESRGAN visually provide better depictions than others in Figure~\ref{fig:3fig .}
elements.sent-2411-157: We investigate the inference time and parameters of recent diffusion-based super-resolution in our workstation with two NVIDIA-RTX ADA-6000 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-158: For a fair comparison, we utilize the same image tensor ($128\times 128\times 3$).
elements.sent-2411-159: The model parameters and inference time of OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff , SinSR~\cite{SinSR , ResShift~\cite{resshift and ours is reported in Table~\ref{tab:complexity .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: HF-Diff can super-resolve images faster than OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff and ResShift~\cite{resshift .}}
elements.sent-2411-162: The effectiveness of the proposed high-frequency perceptual loss can be deduced by both visual and quantitative comparison.
elements.sent-2411-163: From ~Figure~\ref{fig:abalation , we can deduce that HF-perceptual loss improves the texture and fine-grained feature of the real-world sample images more than the SinSR~\cite{SinSR .}}
elements.sent-2411-164: Furthermore, this loss improves the MUSIQ and the CLIPIQA scores between the two samples, as seen in Figure~\ref{fig:abalation .}
elements.sent-2411-165: From Table~\ref{ablation-bismillah , we can infer that HF-perceptual loss significantly improves the NIQE, MUSIQ, and CLIPIQA scores in the DIV2K-Val, RealSet65, and RealSR test sets.}
elements.sent-2411-167: During training, we impose a constraint to match and align the ground truth and super-resolution image distribution in the DINO-v2 space.
elements.sent-2411-168: Figure~\ref{fig:abalation and Table~\ref{ablation-bismillah depict the importance of distribution matching in our HF-Diff method.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: From Figure~\ref{fig:abalation , we can deduce that distribution matching improves the MUSIQ and the CLIPIQA scores more than the vanilla SinSR.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Also, this figure visually depicts that the distribution matching improves the image quality.
elements.sent-2411-171: From Table~\ref{ablation-bismillah , we can deduce that the distribution matching constraint improves the NIQE, CLIPIQA, and MUSIQ scores in all three datasets~\cite{realsr,div2k-val,resshift .}}
elements.sent-2411-172: In summary, we can see that distribution matching is an important component in our HF-Diff super-resolution algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-173: Effectiveness of utilizing the HF-perceptual loss and distribution matching simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-174: We will visually and quantitatively investigate the importance of HF-perceptual loss and distribution matching simultaneously during training, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:abalation and Table~\ref{ablation-bismillah .}}
elements.sent-2411-175: From Figure~\ref{fig:abalation , we can see that both objectives improve the texture and detailed information of the two sample images.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Moreover, from Table~\ref{ablation-bismillah , we can infer that both constraints improve the CLIPIQA score significantly in two real-world~\cite{realsr,resshift and one synthetic~\cite{div2k-val datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Effectiveness of high-frequency perceptual loss compared to LPIPS and VGG-based perceptual loss.
elements.sent-2411-178: Table~\ref{ablation-bismillah shows that, in three datasets, our proposed HF-perceptual loss outperformed the three image quality metrics compared to LPIPS~\cite{lpips and VGG-based perceptual loss~\cite{perceptual_loss_vgg .}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: For a fair comparison, we use the same settings and iteration number for all variations models in the ablation study.

All the approaches are inferred in the same resource setting (NVIDIA-ADA-6000 GPU) and resolution of $128\times128$.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}c|cccc@{}} ResShift~\cite{resshift} SinSR~\cite{SinSR} OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff} Ours{ (HF-Diff)} Inference Step 15 1 1 1 Inference Time (s) 0.741 {0.089} { 0.180} {0.089} \# Trainable Param (M) 118.6 118.6 8.5 118.6 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:complexity}
elements.sent-2411-180: By introducing our high-frequency perceptual loss and distribution alignment in the diffusion-based SR method, we achieve SOTA CLIPIQA~\cite{clipiqa scores in several datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-181: We assume this novel perceptual loss can be employed in other super-resolution algorithms~\cite{resshift,sr-gan for better texture and detail feature generation.}
elements.sent-2411-182: The OSEDiff~\cite{oseDiff utilizes a large-scale pretrained text-to-image diffusion model as a prior that facilitates better MUSIQ~\cite{musiq and NIQE~\cite{niqe scores than ours but has a slower inference speed and lower CLIPIQA score, as shown in Table~\ref{tab-osed and~\ref{tab:complexity .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: We introduce novel perceptual loss to preserve high-frequency features during image super-resolution.
elements.sent-2411-184: For this purpose, we develop an invertible neural network-based high-frequency feature extractor.
elements.sent-2411-185: We also demonstrate that the high-frequency feature extractor captures different edges and detailed information.
elements.sent-2411-186: We also observe high-frequency perceptual loss attains better SR image quality than LPIPS and naive perceptual losses.
elements.sent-2411-187: Furthermore, we align and match the distribution of ground-truth and super-resolution images, which also improves the perceptual quality and visualization of super-resolution images.
elements.sent-2411-188: Introducing these novelties allows the single-step diffusion-based super-resolution model to achieve the state-of-the-art performance on several image-quality metrics.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
